title: Wax Crayon on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Crayon is paraffin wax (petroleum hydrocarbon) + concentrated pigment (iron oxides for earth tones, organic dyes for brights). On upholstery, crayon leaves a physical wax deposit PLUS a pigment stain — two problems in one spot. The wax is the same chemistry as candle wax (freeze + solvent), but crayons load much more pigment than candles, so removing the wax often reveals a color stain underneath that's a separate job. Common mistakes: (1) wiping warm crayon, which smears wax in both directions, (2) ironing synthetic upholstery, which can melt the fabric, and (3) the oily-DIY family (peanut butter, WD-40, mayo, hair conditioner) that softens wax but replaces it with a grease halo on fabric that can't be flushed like clothing. Clean answer: freeze the wax brittle, crack it off, use citrus solvent for residue, then treat any remaining pigment as a separate dye stain.
whyThisWorks: Freezing makes paraffin brittle so bulk lifts off mechanically. Goo Gone / POG dissolves residual wax + provides a solvent pathway to the trapped pigment. Dawn on damp cloth emulsifies the solvent. For residual dye color, 3% peroxide or dilute rubbing alcohol (for lighter / small spots) handles what's left. Upholstery code determines whether water rinse is allowed.
defaultAssumption: Kid coloring on the couch / chair / car seat — extremely common. Fresh crayon (still chunky) = freeze and lift. Worked into the weave (smeared) = harder, more solvent passes. Heat-set crayon (kid sat on it in a warm room, something warm near it) = the wax has melted INTO the fiber and the pigment has set — pro job.

spottingProtocol.technique: Read tag, don't smear warm crayon
spottingProtocol.instruction: Upholstery code: W / W-S = full protocol. S-only = solvent only (no Dawn + water rinse). X = vacuum only, pro. Do NOT wipe the crayon at room temp — warm crayon smears and makes the stain bigger. If cushion cover zips off and is machine-washable, freeze + scrape off the wax first, then wash the cover cold.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Ice in sealed plastic bag, plastic card / dull blade
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press a sealed bag of ice on the crayon. 10–15 min. Never direct ice — water damage. Wax gets hard and brittle.
Crack the wax with the edge of a plastic card. Lift off the bulk pieces. Re-freeze if it softens as you work. Don't gouge.

spottingProtocol.agent: Goo Gone or POG for Residue
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone / POG on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply to cloth, never to upholstery. Tamp the residual wax film from the top. Flip cloth as it picks up wax + pigment. Work outside-in. 2–3 passes. Ventilate — flammable.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: W / W-S: Dawn on damp cloth, work with soft toothbrush, blot. S-only: dry buff residue instead. Damp cloth only, never pour.

spottingProtocol.technique: Separate dye treatment after wax is fully gone
spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth (W/W-S) OR IPA on cloth (S)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Once wax is gone, color often remains. Hidden-area test first. W/W-S code: 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth 5 min. S-only code: 91% isopropyl alcohol on cloth, tamp from top. Pigment is now the only remaining problem.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan-dry. Inspect in daylight. Ghost remaining = one more round of peroxide or IPA on pigment.

steps: 1. Code check. Don't wipe warm crayon.
3. Crack and scrape off with plastic card.
4. Goo Gone / POG tamp from top.
5. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) or dry buff (S).
6. 3% H₂O₂ or IPA for residual pigment (code-dependent).
warnings: Don't wipe warm crayon — smears the wax
NEVER peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo / conditioner — grease halo
NEVER iron synthetic upholstery — fabric melt risk
Pigment is a separate stain after wax is gone

homeSolutions.agent: Don't Wipe, Don't Panic — and Don't Iron
homeSolutions.instruction: Two things NOT to do first: (1) Don't wipe the crayon with a cloth while it's warm — warm crayon smears and makes the stain bigger. (2) Don't iron synthetic upholstery. The "iron + paper bag" trick from viral internet advice works on cotton fabric but NOT on upholstery — most modern upholstery is synthetic and can melt under a hot iron. Also DON'T use peanut butter, WD-40, or mayo — those soften crayon wax but leave a grease ring on upholstery that's worse than the crayon. Clean answer: freeze it.

Tag under a cushion. W / W-S = full protocol. S-only = solvent-only endgame, no Dawn + water rinse. X = vacuum only, pro. If the cushion cover zips off and is machine-washable, freeze and scrape the crayon off first, then wash the cover cold — much easier than treating in place.

homeSolutions.agent: Freeze the Crayon Brittle — 10 to 15 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Put ice in a zip-lock bag, press it directly on the crayon for 10–15 minutes. NEVER put ice directly on the upholstery — water damage. The wax will freeze brittle.

Once frozen, crack the wax with the edge of a plastic card, credit card, or dull butter knife. Lift off the pieces. Most of the bulk comes off cleanly. If the wax starts softening, refreeze. Work gently — don't gouge the fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or POG on a White Rag for Residue
homeSolutions.instruction: A thin wax film usually remains in the weave. Put Goo Gone (orange bottle, hardware/grocery store) on a clean white rag — NEVER pour on upholstery. Press and tamp the residue area from the TOP. Flip the rag as it picks up wax and pigment. Work outside-in. Open windows — Goo Gone is flammable, no flame.

homeSolutions.agent: Clear the Solvent — Dawn or Dry Buff Depending on Code
homeSolutions.instruction: W or W-S upholstery: work a drop of Dawn with cool water into a damp cloth, press on the spot 5 min to clear the Goo Gone, blot. S-only code: DON'T add water. Dry-buff the solvent residue with a clean cloth instead. Either way, no pouring.

Once the wax is gone, you'll often see the crayon's pigment color (red, blue, purple) still in the fabric. That's now a dye stain, not a wax stain. Hidden-area test first. W / W-S upholstery: dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a damp white cloth, press 5 min, blot. S-only upholstery: dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth, tamp the color from the top. Dark crayons (red, purple, black) may need 2–3 rounds.
Stack a dry towel on the spot with a heavy book for 30 minutes. Fan-dry until fully dry. Inspect in daylight. Residual color = repeat step 7.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — Melted-In, X-Code, or Delicate Fiber
homeSolutions.instruction: If someone already ironed it and the wax melted into the fabric, if the code is X or the upholstery is leather / silk / wool, or if color persists after 2 rounds on pigment: pro. Tell them: "Crayon on [W / W-S / S / X / unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / melted-in], home-treated with freeze + Goo Gone + [Dawn] + [3% peroxide OR 91% IPA]. [Residual color / melted into fiber / delicate fabric]." Don't mention peanut butter even if someone tried it — just note what you actually used.

steps: 1. Don't wipe warm crayon. Don't iron. Don't use oily DIY hacks.
4. Crack and lift with plastic card.
5. Goo Gone on rag, tamp from top. Ventilate.
6. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) OR dry buff (S).
7. 3% peroxide (W/W-S) OR 91% IPA (S) for residual pigment.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if crayon has been ironed into the fiber, upholstery is X-code or leather/silk/wool, or color persists after 2 pigment rounds.
warnings: NEVER wipe warm crayon — smears wax
NEVER iron synthetic upholstery — melts fiber
NEVER peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo / conditioner — grease halo on fabric
Never pour solvent or water on upholstery

neverDo: Never oily DIY tricks on fabric — grease halo
Never pour solvent or water on upholstery
Never use solvent near open flame
fiberSensitivities: W / W-S: tolerates freeze + Goo Gone + Dawn + 3% peroxide
S-only: freeze + Goo Gone + 91% IPA, no water rinse
Silk / wool: pro, pigment chemistry varies

materialWarnings: Ironing upholstery melts most synthetic fabrics
Oily DIY hacks replace crayon with grease
Pigment is a SEPARATE stain after wax is gone

escalation.when: Crayon has been ironed into fabric (wax melted in), X-code upholstery, leather, silk, wool, or residual pigment persists after 2 rounds on fiber-tested code-appropriate chemistry.
escalation.whatToTell: "Crayon on [W / W-S / S / X / unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / melted-in], home-treated with freeze + Goo Gone [+ Dawn / + IPA / + 3% peroxide]. [Residual pigment / melted-in wax / delicate fiber]. No oily DIY tricks used."
escalation.specialistType: professional upholstery cleaner with solvent spotting

contextQuestions: Upholstery code — W, W-S, S, X, unknown?
Does the cushion cover zip off and is it machine-washable?
Has anyone already ironed it or tried peanut butter / WD-40?
Fresh crayon or melted-in (warm room, sat on, etc.)?
Crayon color — light or dark (red / purple / black hardest)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The iron-method is dangerous on synthetic upholstery — don't do it. The oily-DIY-hack family leaves permanent grease halos on fabric. Freeze-and-solvent + separate pigment treatment is the clean path. Melted-in crayon (kid sat on it, warm room) is much harder because wax penetrated fiber — often a pro job.
